notes.note: The letter-heading. The recipient is Aulus Allienus, a Roman officer here addressed as a provincial governor (he is more fully styled proconsul in the companion letter 13.79). "S." abbreviates salutem (dicit), "sends greetings."

"Host" renders hospes, a partner in hospitium: the formal, heritable bond of guest-friendship by which a Roman and a foreigner each owed the other lodging, protection, and good offices. It is the standing relationship that underwrites this letter of recommendation.

notes.anchor: very nearly the foremost man of Achaea
notes.note: Achaea is the Greek mainland south of Macedonia (Athens, Corinth, the Peloponnese); Sicyon, Democritus's city, lay within it. To call a man princeps (foremost) of the region marks him as a leading provincial notable whose goodwill a governor would value.

notes.anchor: you receive him into your good faith
notes.note: To receive someone in fidem is to take him formally under one's protection and good faith - the core act Cicero requests in his letters of recommendation, binding the recipient to look after the man's interests as if they were the writer's own.
